title: Islamic World
subtitle: From Al-Andalus to Samarkand — geometry, calligraphy, tile, and dome.
intro: Islamic art spans three continents and fourteen centuries. Without figural imagery at its centre, it found other languages: endless pattern, abstract color, script as image, and vast cool interiors that turn geometry into prayer.

works.title: Dome of the Rock
works.desc: The earliest surviving major Islamic building. The gold dome marks the rock from which Muhammad is said to have ascended on the Night Journey.

works.title: Mezquita of Córdoba
works.desc: A forest of red-and-white striped arches from a great caliphate. A cathedral was dropped into the middle of it in the 16th century.

works.title: Hagia Sophia
works.desc: A Byzantine cathedral that held the largest dome on earth for a thousand years, then an Ottoman mosque, then a museum, now a mosque again.

works.title: Alhambra
works.desc: A palace-citadel of courtyards, fountains, and muqarnas ceilings. The poems inscribed on the walls are part of the architecture.

works.title: Registan of Samarkand
works.desc: Three madrasas framing a public square — Ulugh Beg's was home to one of the greatest astronomical observatories of the medieval world.

works.title: Topkapı Palace
works.desc: The sultans' residence for 400 years. Unlike European palaces, it's a scatter of pavilions in a walled garden — closer to a fortified village.

works.title: Süleymaniye Mosque
works.desc: Mimar Sinan's masterpiece from the middle of his career — the architect's own tomb is in the garden outside.

works.title: Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed)
works.desc: The Ottoman answer to Hagia Sophia across the square. More than 20,000 İznik tiles line the interior — the blue that gave it its Western name.

works.title: Shah Mosque, Isfahan
works.desc: Seven-colour tilework on the scale of a whole square. Inside the dome, one clap echoes seven times.

works.title: Ardabil Carpet
works.desc: Medallion carpet with 25 million knots. Made as a pair — the other half sold off piece by piece to repair this one. Now in the V&A.

works.title: İznik Pottery
works.desc: The workshop town that supplied tiles for mosques from Istanbul to Damascus. The signature tomato-red slip was a short-lived chemical triumph.

works.title: Great Mosque of Kairouan
works.desc: The oldest purpose-built mosque still standing. Its square minaret became the model for North African and Andalusi mosques for a thousand years.

works.title: Umayyad Mosque of Damascus
works.desc: Built on a site already sacred to Jupiter, John the Baptist, and Mary. Byzantine mosaicists were brought in to lay the gold-ground interior.

works.title: Qutb Minar
works.desc: The tallest brick minaret in the world — 238 feet of red sandstone, carved with Qur'anic calligraphy as deeply as architecture.

works.title: Mosque of Ibn Tulun
works.desc: The oldest mosque in Cairo still in its original form. A vast brick courtyard under a spiral minaret — a shape borrowed from Samarra.

works.title: Po-i-Kalyan
works.desc: A minaret, mosque, and madrasa across one square. Tradition says Genghis Khan spared the minaret because his hat fell off as he looked up at it.
